title: Data Binding
subtitle: Connect your TypeScript class to the HTML template
description: Master all four types of Angular data binding — interpolation, property binding, event binding, and two-way binding. Plus class/style binding and template reference variables.
keyPoints: Interpolation {{ expr }} renders TypeScript expressions directly in the template
Property binding [prop]="value" pushes data FROM the class INTO the DOM
Event binding (event)="handler()" listens to DOM events and calls class methods
Two-way binding [(ngModel)] combines property + event binding in one shorthand
Class binding [class.name]="bool" and style binding [style.prop]="value" toggle CSS dynamically
Template reference variables #ref give you a direct handle to a DOM element or component
Angular uses one-way data flow by default — this makes apps predictable and easy to debug

sections.title: What Is Data Binding?
sections.content:
Data binding is the mechanism that keeps your TypeScript class (the data) and your HTML template (the view) in sync. Without data binding you would have to manually read from and write to the DOM using JavaScript — Angular automates all of that.

There are four distinct types of data binding in Angular, each serving a different direction of data flow:

① Interpolation       — class → template (read-only text)
② Property Binding    — class → DOM element property
③ Event Binding       — DOM event → class method
④ Two-way Binding     — class ↔ DOM (both directions at once)

sections.caption: Interpolation and Property Binding push data from class → template. Event Binding pulls events from template → class. Two-way binding does both simultaneously.

sections.title: ① Interpolation — {{ expression }}
Interpolation Examples
sections.explanation: Signals use the same {{ }} syntax — just add () to read the value. Angular tracks signal reads inside templates automatically.

sections.content: Keep interpolation expressions simple. If you need complex logic, move it into a computed signal or a method in the class. Templates should describe the UI, not contain business logic.

sections.title: ② Property Binding — [property]="expression"
sections.content:
Property binding sets a DOM element's property to a value from the class. It uses square brackets [ ] around the property name.

The key difference between property binding and HTML attributes:
• HTML attributes (src="...") are static strings set once at parse time
• Property binding [src]="..." is dynamic — Angular evaluates the expression and updates the DOM property whenever the value changes

Common uses:
• [src]="imageUrl"         — image source
• [href]="linkUrl"         — anchor href
• [disabled]="isLoading"   — button disabled state
• [value]="inputValue"     — input value
• [hidden]="isHidden"      — hide/show element
• [class]="cssClass"       — set full class string

sections.title: Property Binding in Action
sections.explanation: [disabled]="isLoading()" passes a boolean — when isLoading() is true the button is disabled. Without brackets, disabled="isLoading()" would pass the string 'isLoading()' which is always truthy.

sections.title: ③ Event Binding — (event)="handler($event)"
sections.content:
Event binding listens to DOM events and calls a method in your class when they fire. You wrap the event name in parentheses ( ).

Common events:
• (click)        — mouse click
• (input)        — input field value changes
• (change)       — select/checkbox changes
• (submit)       — form submission
• (keydown)      — key pressed
• (keydown.enter) — specifically the Enter key
• (mouseenter) / (mouseleave) — hover events
• (focus) / (blur) — focus events

The special $event variable holds the native DOM event object. For input elements, $event.target.value gives you the current text.

sections.title: Event Binding Examples
sections.explanation: Always call event.preventDefault() on (submit) — without it, the browser reloads the page. For (keydown.enter), Angular filters the event for you automatically.

sections.content: Avoid complex logic in event binding expressions. (click)="count.set(0)" is fine, but (click)="users.filter(u => u.active).map(u => u.id).forEach(...)" belongs in a class method. Keep templates readable.

sections.title: ④ Two-Way Binding — [(ngModel)]
sections.content:
Two-way binding combines property binding and event binding into a single shorthand syntax: [(ngModel)]. It is often called the "banana in a box" because of its shape: [( )].

Under the hood, [(ngModel)]="name" is exactly equivalent to:
• [ngModel]="name"           — property binding (sets the input value from the class)
• (ngModelChange)="name=$event" — event binding (updates the class when the input changes)

Important: ngModel requires FormsModule to be imported in your component's imports array.

Two-way binding is best for simple form inputs. For complex forms with validation, use ReactiveFormsModule instead.

sections.title: Two-Way Binding with ngModel
sections.code:
import { Component } from '@angular/core'; import { FormsModule } from '@angular/forms'; // required! @Component({ selector: 'app-profile-form', standalone: true, imports: [FormsModule], // import FormsModule here template: `

Hello, {{ username }}!

Role: {{ role }}

Subscribed: {{ newsletter }}

`, }) export class ProfileFormComponent { username = 'Angular Dev'; role = 'user'; newsletter = false; }
sections.explanation: FormsModule must be in the component's imports array. Without it, Angular throws 'Can't bind to ngModel since it isn't a known property of input'.

sections.caption: [(ngModel)] is syntactic sugar — Angular expands it into separate property and event bindings behind the scenes.

sections.title: Class Binding and Style Binding
sections.content:
Beyond standard property binding, Angular provides shorthand syntaxes for toggling CSS classes and inline styles dynamically.

Class binding:
• [class.active]="isActive"        — adds 'active' class when isActive is truthy
• [class]="'btn btn-primary'"      — sets the entire class attribute as a string
• [ngClass]="{ active: isActive, hidden: isHidden }" — object map (needs NgClass)

Style binding:
• [style.color]="textColor"          — sets a single CSS property
• [style.font-size.px]="fontSize"    — with a unit suffix (.px, .em, .%)
• [ngStyle]="{ color: textColor }"   — object map (needs NgStyle)

Class and style bindings are pure convenience — under the hood they are property bindings on the element's classList and style objects.

sections.title: Class & Style Binding
sections.explanation: [style.width.%]="progress()" automatically appends the '%' unit. You can use .px, .em, .rem, .%, .vh, .vw as suffixes.

sections.title: Template Reference Variables — #name
sections.content:
A template reference variable gives you a direct TypeScript reference to a DOM element, Angular component, or directive inside your template, without needing ViewChild in the class.

You declare one with a # prefix on any element: <input #myInput />. Then you can:
• Read properties in the same template: {{ myInput.value }}
• Pass it to methods: (click)="submit(myInput.value)"
• Reference a component: <app-form #formRef /> — gives you the component instance

Template reference variables are local to the template — you cannot access them in the TypeScript class directly. For that, use @ViewChild.

sections.title: Template Reference Variables
sections.explanation: #loginForm="ngForm" assigns the NgForm directive (not the raw element) to the variable — this is how you get access to Angular's form validation state inside the template.

sections.content: Template reference variables are evaluated at render time. If you reference a variable before it is rendered (e.g., inside an @if block that starts hidden), the variable will be undefined until the element appears. Use ViewChild with { static: false } in the class for such cases.

sections.title: Putting It All Together
sections.content:
Real Angular templates combine all four binding types naturally. Here is a typical search component using every type of binding:

• Interpolation to display results count
• Property binding to disable the button while loading
• Event binding to handle input and button clicks
• Two-way binding on the search field
• Class binding to highlight active filters
• Template reference variable for the input element

sections.title: All Binding Types Combined
sections.explanation: Notice how query is a plain string (not a signal) because [(ngModel)] requires a regular writable property. Signals can't be used directly with ngModel — use a plain property or handle it with (ngModelChange).

sections.content: Angular 17+ signals and [(ngModel)] don't mix directly. If you want reactive search with signals, use (ngModelChange)="query.set($event)" and [ngModel]="query()" instead of the shorthand [(ngModel)].

interviewQuestions.q: What are the four types of data binding in Angular and what direction does data flow in each?
interviewQuestions.a: ① Interpolation {{ }} — class → template (read-only, renders text). ② Property binding [prop]="expr" — class → DOM property (dynamic attribute values). ③ Event binding (event)="handler()" — DOM event → class method (reacts to user actions). ④ Two-way binding [(ngModel)] — class ↔ DOM (bidirectional, combines ② and ③). Only event binding has data flowing from the template back to the class.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the difference between an HTML attribute and a DOM property? Why does this matter for property binding?
interviewQuestions.a: HTML attributes are what you write in the source HTML — they initialise DOM properties but don't update them. DOM properties are the live, current state of the element. For example, the value attribute sets the initial value of an input; after the user types, the DOM property value reflects the current text but the attribute still holds the initial value. Property binding [value]="x" always targets the live DOM property, so it stays in sync with your data.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the 'banana in a box' and what does [(ngModel)] expand to?
interviewQuestions.a: The 'banana in a box' is a nickname for the two-way binding syntax [(ngModel)], because [( )] looks like a banana inside a box. It expands to a property binding plus an event binding: [ngModel]="name" (sets the input's value from the class) combined with (ngModelChange)="name = $event" (updates the class when the user types). They are exactly equivalent, the shorthand just saves you from writing both.

interviewQuestions.q: What module must be imported to use ngModel, and where do you import it?
interviewQuestions.a: FormsModule from @angular/forms. With standalone components (Angular 17+), you add it to the imports array of the @Component decorator: imports: [FormsModule]. Without it Angular throws: 'Can't bind to ngModel since it isn't a known property of input'.

interviewQuestions.q: What is $event in event binding and how is it typed?
interviewQuestions.a: $event is the native DOM event object emitted by the event. Its type depends on the event — (click) gives MouseEvent, (input) gives InputEvent, (keydown) gives KeyboardEvent, (submit) gives SubmitEvent. When a component emits via output(), $event is the value passed to emit(). You should cast $event.target to the appropriate element type (e.g., event.target as HTMLInputElement) to get typed access to .value.

interviewQuestions.q: What is a template reference variable and how is it different from ViewChild?
interviewQuestions.a: A template reference variable (#name) is a local alias declared in the template that refers to a DOM element, component, or directive. It can only be used within the same template — you can't access it in the TypeScript class. ViewChild is a class-level decorator (@ViewChild) that gives the TypeScript class a reference to a child element or component, available after ngAfterViewInit. Use template references for simple in-template interactions; use ViewChild when you need to call methods or read values in the class.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you conditionally add a CSS class in Angular without using ngClass?
interviewQuestions.a: Use class binding: [class.your-class]="booleanExpression". For example, [class.active]="isActive()" adds the 'active' class when the expression is truthy and removes it when falsy. This is the simplest and most readable option for toggling a single class. For multiple classes based on conditions, ngClass with an object map { 'class-a': condA, 'class-b': condB } is more convenient.

interviewQuestions.q: Can you use Angular Signals directly with [(ngModel)]?
interviewQuestions.a: No — [(ngModel)] requires a plain writable class property, not a signal. A signal is a function, so [(ngModel)]="mySignal" would try to two-way bind to the function itself, not its value. Instead, split it: [ngModel]="mySignal()" (read the signal value) and (ngModelChange)="mySignal.set($event)" (write back on change). Or use a plain property and update the signal manually in the event handler.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the difference between [class]="expr" and [class.name]="bool"?
interviewQuestions.a: [class]="expr" replaces the entire className string with the result of the expression — useful when you compute the full class list. [class.name]="bool" adds or removes just one named class based on the boolean, leaving all other classes unchanged. The single-class form is usually safer since it doesn't accidentally remove classes added by other bindings or static class attributes.

interviewQuestions.q: What happens if you write (click)="doSomething" without the parentheses?
interviewQuestions.a: Without parentheses — (click)="doSomething" — you are not calling the method; you are assigning the method reference to the event handler. Angular will not throw an error, but when the click fires, nothing visible will happen because doSomething is a function reference, not an invocation. Always write (click)="doSomething()" with parentheses to actually call the method.
